items.q: What does a liquidity ratio like the current ratio measure?
items.correct: A business's ability to meet short-term obligations — current assets divided by current liabilities. A ratio above 1 means assets cover liabilities; too high may indicate poor use of resources.
items.misconception: A higher liquidity ratio is always better.
items.random_wrong: Liquidity ratios measure how quickly a business sells products.
items.why: Very high ratios can signal idle cash or excessive stock — capital that could earn returns. Liquidity is about meeting bills on time, not maximising assets. Students assume bigger is better and miss the trade-off.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: Acid test ratio strips out which item?
items.why: Correct answer: Stock. “Cash” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A current ratio below 1 usually signals:
items.why: It's Liquidity stress. “Excess liquidity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: CA = 300, stock = 100, CL = 100. Acid test ratio?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
